query: insulin effect on congenital diseases in diabetes

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: As discussed above, MODY is an autosomal dominant condition, and different genetic subtypes behave differently. Patient education is important in terms of teaching them that it runs in families, and thus genetic counseling becomes vital. Knowing that this condition runs in families can allow for adequate screening and implementation of treatment, if necessary, early to avoid complications. They must understand the disease and the need to follow up. Even though certain subtypes may only require dietary intervention, the disease may progress to the point of necessitating pharmacologic intervention. Certain subtypes have a higher propensity of developing complications, and therefore counseling patients on regular appropriate screenings and subspecialist follow-up is important. Women with MODY and the GCK mutation need to be made aware that they may require insulin treatment during pregnancy depending on the fetus genotype, and they should be monitored closely by a specialist during their pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: For infants with persistent hypoglycemia, the goal of management in infants with suspected hyperinsulinism is to prevent recurrent episodes of low blood glucose levels that put the infant at risk for future episodes of hypoglycemia. For neonates with inborn errors of metabolism and impaired endogenous glucose production, the goal of management is to prevent metabolic acidosis and subsequent growth failure. [4] The Pediatric Endocrine Society recommends giving endogenous glucose to keep blood glucose levels >70 mg/dL in these infants. Diazoxide infusions of 10 to 15 mg/kg per day may be used in infants with congenital hyperinsulinism to inhibit insulin secretions; effects are seen within 2 to 4 days. [12] Recommendations call for early consultation with endocrinology or genetics. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Treatment / Management -- Management of MODY in the Youth
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned above, GCK MODY generally does not require treatment except during pregnancy. Determining which GCK MODY patients need treatment is determined by the fetal genotype. This is not genetically determined but assessed by the abdominal circumference of the fetus measured during the second trimester. Should this value exceed 75%, this infers that the fetus does not carry the GCK mutation, and thus these mothers should be treated with insulin during pregnancy to help prevent macrosomia. If the fetus has an abdominal circumference less than 75%, this infers the fetus does not carry the gene as they will have the same glucose setpoint as the mother, which is increased, and thus maternal hyperglycemia will be sensed by the fetus as normal and therefore growth will be normal and maternal treatment with insulin is not indicated, and it can even result in growth restriction. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Diabetic Hypoglycemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Infants and children with hypoglycemia usually have an inborn error of metabolism (IEM), and it is associated with other features including hepatomegaly (glycogen storage disease), seizures (hereditary fructose intolerance), weight loss, vomiting and cataract (galactosemia), and short stature (growth hormone deficiency). [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Hexokinase Method -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The causes of hypoglycemia are different in different age groups. Small for gestational age or prematurity is one of the common causes of hypoglycemia in neonates. Respiratory distress syndrome, infections, and maternal gestational diabetes mellitus can also lead to hypoglycemia in neonates. [43] Inborn errors of carbohydrate metabolisms, such as glycogen storage diseases, galactosemia, hereditary fructose intolerance, and malnourished or neglected children are causes of hypoglycemia in infants. Alcohol, liver diseases, insulinoma, skipped meals after insulin or oral hypoglycaemic agent, and Addison disease (glucocorticoid deficiency) are common causes of hypoglycemia in adults. [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: History should first include a thorough personal medical history. The diabetes diagnosis characteristic of MODY occurs in adolescence or early adulthood. Therefore details surrounding their diabetes diagnosis become of utmost importance. Also, birth history can provide some helpful information if known. Intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) is seen in those with HNF1B (MODY 5) in addition to some congenital abnormalities such as renal and urogenital tract anomalies as well as pancreatic hypoplasia. Those with the HNF4A (MODY 1) subtype may have a history of a birth weight generally more than 800gr above normal, as well as transient neonatal hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. GCK (MODY 2) patients may have had a history of mild fasting hyperglycemia at birth. Finally, some of those with HFN1A (MODY 3) may have had transient neonatal hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia. [17] A further personal medical history should include a full review of systems and enquire about known medical problems involving other organ systems as some forms of MODY can be associated with extra-beta cell manifestations, including renal, hepatic, genitourinary, exocrine pancreatic, or intestinal effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucose Metabolism -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Fetal insulin secretion gradually increases during the gestational period. Pulsatile peaks in glucose levels are beneficial to insulin secretion; however, constant hyperglycemia down-regulates insulin sensitivity and glucose tolerance. [4] In mothers with elevated serum glucose levels, adverse fetal impacts include congenital abnormalities, fetal macrosomia, and stillbirth. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Maternal diabetes can adversely affect embryogenesis and fetal development, resulting in multiple congenital anomalies and secondary medical complications collectively termed as "diabetic embryopathy." High maternal blood glucose acts as a major teratogenic agent by altering many normal signaling pathways involved in fetal development and organogenesis. This condition is most commonly associated with pregestational diabetes mellitus (type 1 and type 2), although poorly controlled gestational diabetes can also contribute to teratogenic outcomes if hyperglycemia occurs early in pregnancy. The incidence of congenital anomalies is significantly higher in infants born to diabetic mothers compared to the general population, thereby underscoring the teratogenic risk of maternal hyperglycemia during early embryonic development.

retrieved_docs.title: Kabuki Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Endocrinologic
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoglycemia presents in up to 28% of patients with Kabuki syndrome in either the neonatal period or later in life. [8] Persistent hypoglycemia may occur in neonates due to hyperinsulinism and may be a presenting sign of the syndrome. There is an estimation that approximately 1% of neonates with hyperinsulinism have KS. [5] Patients with KDM6A mutations have an increased risk of hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia due to the effect of KDM6A on insulin release from beta-cells of the pancreas. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: MODY is caused by defects in pancreatic islet cell development and insulin secretion. It is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, and the patients generally have heterozygote mutations. Penetrance and expressivity can vary immensely, even among family members. MODY genes affect insulin secretion via impairment of insulin sensing, glucose metabolism in beta cells, or activation of adenosine triphosphate (ATP)-dependent potassium channels. The disorder is best managed by an interprofessional team that consists of a diabetic nurse, endocrinologist, internist, geneticist, dietitian, ophthalmologist, nephrologist, occasionally gynecologist (MODY 5), and a cardiologist. These patients are prone to the same complications as other diabetics except for patients with the GCK mutation, and early referral to a specialist is highly recommended. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Pathophysiology -- ABCC8 (MODY 12) and K CNJ11 (MODY 13)
retrieved_docs.content: The ABCC8 and KCNJ1 mutations have been associated with a spectrum of disorders, including neonatal diabetes.[9] Neonatal diabetes can be permanent or transient, in which case it often recurs later in life. Neonatal diabetes has been associated with mutations in the potassium-ATP channel genes or mutations in the insulin gene. [5] Additionally, these mutations have been linked to neonatal hypoglycemia due to elevated insulin levels; this is usually transient, but the patient may develop diabetes later in life. In these cases, monitoring of fetal growth during pregnancy and blood glucose levels in neonates can be crucial. The patients who develop diabetes generally respond well to sulfonylureas.

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Embryopathy -- Pathophysiology -- Other Glucose By-Products
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperglycemia disrupts the uptake of inositol by embryonic tissues. Depletion of myo-inositol, especially during critical stages of embryogenesis, has been associated with the development of congenital malformations associated with diabetic embryopathy. Recent experimental studies suggest that myo-inositol supplementation may help mitigate some of these adverse effects, although it is not yet included in standard clinical recommendations. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Whereas DM1 and DM2 are polygenic, MODY is caused by a single gene mutation that leads to a defect in beta cell insulin secretion in response to glucose stimulation. Most genetic versions of MODY have autosomal dominant transmission, although, less frequently, autosomal recessive versions may also exist and could account for neonatal diabetes. Initially, different types of MODY were described numerically (MODY 1-6). However, they are now classified by their genetic defect.

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Pathophysiology -- HNF1B (MODY 5)
retrieved_docs.content: The HNF1B mutation (MODY 5) accounts for less than 5% of MODY. It is associated with a wide variation in presentations. Half of these patients present with early-onset DM. This mutation can affect gene regulation in the liver, kidneys, intestines, lungs, or ovaries. Patients can present with abnormalities such as renal cysts, dysplasia, renal tract malformations, or hypoplastic glomerulocystic kidney disease. HNF1B MODY is sometimes referred to as RCAD (renal cysts and diabetes syndrome). There can be a progressive loss of renal function independent of diabetic nephropathy. [15] Patients with HNF1B generally are not responsive to oral medications and will require treatment with insulin.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: DM is a disorder in regulating blood glucose levels and is associated with genetic and lifestyle factors, such as family history, race, obesity, and diet. DM resulting from other causes include monogenic diabetes (such as neonatal diabetes), maturity onset of diabetes in the young (MODY), gestational diabetes, pancreatic insufficiency, or drug-induced. High blood glucose levels characterize the disorder due to insulin resistance and ultimate insulin deficiency. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Insulin Resistance -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Articulate the acquired and genetic causes of insulin resistance. Explain the pathophysiology of insulin resistance. Summarize the 3 arms in the management of insulin resistance. Apply effective processes to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and reduce complications for patients with insulin resistance. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the disease مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are considered manifestations of diabetes. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting the well‑known link between diabetes and retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy. In addition, malfunction of the human INSR protein is gene‑product‑associated with مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Finally, متلازمة الأيض is described as a child condition of مرض السكري, underscoring the close relationship between diabetes and metabolic syndrome in patient care and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is an associated morphology (النَخَر) and is linked

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all identified as specific manifestations or related entities of مرض السكري (diabetes). In clinical practice, مرض السكري is linked not only to its primary metabolic disturbances but also to other pancreatic disorders that are distinct from diabetes itself, highlighting the broader spectrum of pancreatic pathology that may coexist with or mimic diabetic presentations. Additionally, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy and other vision‑related complications in patients with diabetes. Furthermore, مرض السكري is recognized as having a child condition, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome), underscoring the interrelationship between diabetes and the cluster of metabolic risk factors that include obesity, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance. Together, these connections emphasize the heterogeneous nature of diabetes, its potential neonatal and developmental presentations, its association with other pancreatic and ocular disorders, and its role as a central component of metabolic syndrome, all of which are important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide spectrum of clinical conditions and genetic factors. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2, as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, indicating that early‑onset glucose dysregulation can coexist with neurodevelopmental abnormalities. Dysfunction of the human INSR protein is identified as a gene‑product malfunction that is associated with the development of diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disorder. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several serious complications affecting multiple organ systems. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are both noted as conditions that commonly co‑occur with diabetes, reflecting its impact on vascular and cardiac health. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is listed as a child

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to مرض السكري (diabetes), indicating that various forms of neonatal diabetes and associated neurodevelopmental disorders are classified under the broader disease of diabetes. The human INSR protein, when its function is impaired, is linked to the pathogenesis of مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin‑receptor signaling in the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the common ocular complications of diabetes. Pharmacologically, the contraceptive agent desogestrel and the medication ethynodioldiacetát are contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, underscoring the need to avoid these drugs in diabetic care. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is described as a child of مرض السكري, indicating a close interrelationship in which metabolic syndrome often coexists with or progresses to diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, genetic factors, comorbidities, and therapeutic considerations relevant to diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms of diabetes that map directly to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also a manifestation of مرض السكري, representing gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance within the broader diabetes spectrum. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this emergency contraceptive should be avoided in individuals with diabetes. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy in diabetic patients. Finally, malfunction of the human INSR protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of مرض السكري, underscoring the role of insulin‑receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, contraindications, complications, and molecular mechanisms associated with diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 571
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that serves as the parent of the “Diagnosis/Diseases Component.” Several specific forms of neonatal diabetes—including “DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder),” “Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1,” and the syndrome “Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes”—are all mapped to diabetes, underscoring its relevance across age groups. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is identified as a child of diabetes, indicating that metabolic syndrome is considered a sub‑category or consequence of the diabetic condition. Diabetes is clinically associated with a range of comorbid conditions. Pancreatic disorders that are not themselves diabetes, retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), and congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) are all linked to diabetes, highlighting the systemic impact of the disease on both exocrine pancreatic function, ocular health, and cardiac function. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease entity that encompasses a wide range of related conditions, complications, and associated diagnostic components. Several specific forms of diabetes, including DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these neonatal and transient presentations are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Clinically, diabetes is linked to multiple serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني) is also clinically associated with diabetes, and the procedure of amputation of lower extremity is further linked to this cardiac condition, underscoring the systemic impact of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all classified under مرض السكري, indicating that they represent specific forms or presentations of diabetes mellitus, particularly transient neonatal diabetes. The human INSR protein, when its function is impaired, is linked to the pathogenesis of مرض السكري, highlighting the importance of insulin‑receptor signaling in the disease. Clinically, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known ocular complications of diabetes. Moreover, the drugs ethynodioldiacetát and norgestrel are contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, suggesting that their use may worsen glycemic control or interact adversely with the condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 360
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of the lower extremity is a hierarchical surgical category that includes specific procedures such as amputation of a toe (بتر أصبع القدم), below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), and above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة). All of these limb‑loss procedures are clinically associated with diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري), reflecting the strong link between chronic hyperglycemia and peripheral vascular disease that often necessitates amputation. In addition to limb complications, diabetes mellitus is also clinically associated with retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), underscoring the disease’s impact on ocular health. Genetic and molecular factors further connect to diabetes: malfunction of the human insulin receptor protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of diabetes, highlighting the role of insulin signaling defects in disease pathogenesis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1539
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) encompasses several specific entities, including transient neonatal forms such as DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder) and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, as well as neonatal diabetes that co‑occurs with developmental delay and epilepsy (Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes). A pregnancy‑related manifestation, described as diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium, is recognized as a form of the same disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), and the presence of diabetes contraindicates the use of the emergency contraceptive norgestrel. Genetic dysfunction of the human insulin receptor protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the pathogenesis of diabetes. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is considered a downstream condition or “child” of diabetes, reflecting the close metabolic relationship between the two disorders. Both diabetes and metabolic syndrome share a comorbidity identifier (komorbidita), indicating that they frequently co‑occur. Together, these relationships highlight the diverse presentations, genetic contributors, associated complications, medication considerations, and related metabolic conditions that are important for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 360

Several specific forms of diabetes—such as Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1; Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder); and the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes—are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes mellitus, indicating that these neonatal and developmental presentations are recognized subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Pharmacologically, certain agents are contraindicated in patients with diabetes mellitus. Both ethynodioldiacetát and norgestrel are identified as drugs that should be avoided in the context of diabetes, reflecting potential adverse interactions or exacerbation of glycemic control. Complications of amputation are also described. retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all classified as اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, a neonatal disorder. This grouping indicates that both forms of transient neonatal diabetes mellitus and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes fall under the broader category of neonatal disorders, highlighting their importance in newborn assessment, monitoring, and specialized management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2091
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and associated neurodevelopmental presentations are considered manifestations or subtypes of diabetes. The hormonal contraceptive desogestrel and the medication ethynodioldiacetát are contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, reflecting the need to avoid these agents due to potential adverse effects on glycemic control. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, underscoring the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy in diabetic patients. Additionally, metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is identified as a child condition of مرض السكري, highlighting the close metabolic link between insulin resistance, dyslipidemia, and diabetes. Together, these relationships emphasize the diverse clinical spectrum of diabetes, its complications, and important considerations for medication safety and comorbid metabolic disorders.
